romania.title: 66 de câini, dintre care 32 erau pui, preluaţi de ASPA București de pe străzi. Cățeluș de 3 luni, salvat dintr-un canal
romania.summary: Echipele ASPA Bucureşti au preluat de pe domeniul pubic, de la începutul lunii iulie, 66 de câini, dintre care 32 de pui, consecinţă directă a nesterilizării şi a abandonului. Speciaţiştii avertizează că sterilizarea şi microciparea câinilor cu stăpân sunt cele mai eficiente măsuri pentru prevenirea abandonului şi reducerea numărului de animale care ajung pe domeniul public.

romania.title: O nouă țară vecină cu Belarus se confruntă acum cu o creştere a numărului de migranţi la frontieră
romania.summary: Letonia a înregistrat o creştere a numărului de migranţi nedocumentaţi care au încercat să treacă frontiera din Belarus, vecinul său de la Est, a declarat joi şeful poliţiei de frontieră Guntis Pujats pentru postul de radio leton, scrie Agerpres. „Din aprilie, situaţia a fost tensionată şi complicată”, a spus Pujats. El a spus că tentative …

romania.summary: Peste 6.500 de elevi și 400 profesori din 48 de școli din România au finalizat cu succes programul Doers School, creat de Let’s Do It, Romania!. Cea mai mare mișcare… Articolul Peste 6.500 de elevi au absolvit programul Doers School în anul școlar 2025 – 2026, inițiat de Let’s Do It, Romania! apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
romania.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

medical.title: A healthy diet during pregnancy associated with lower exposure to some—but not all—chemicals, study finds
medical.summary: Following dietary guidelines during pregnancy may reduce exposure to many—but not all—environmental chemicals from food and everyday products, according to a new study of nearly 1,500 pregnant participants. The study, "Dietary guidelines adherence and pregnancy exposure to 10 classes of priority chemicals: An observational study in the ECHO Cohort," was published in the American Journal of Clinical Nutrition.

medical.title: Hidden metastases reveal clues to colorectal cancer recurrence
medical.summary: Researchers identified a six-gene signature in microscopic colorectal cancer (CRC) liver metastases that may help predict recurrence after treatment. The findings suggest these tiny, often undetectable tumor deposits could serve as a tissue-based marker of residual cancer cells, recurrence risk and chemotherapy resistance.

medical.title: Investigating AI-based personal training
medical.summary: A feature News and Perspectives story on AI fitness advice has been published in the Journal of Medical Internet Research.. In "Should AI Be Your Personal Trainer?", JMIR Correspondent Anna Zucker covers the growing use of AI chatbots for personal fitness programs.

medical.title: Mouse study identifies C1 neurons as a driver of prolonged fear and anxiety
medical.summary: Anxiety disorders affect more than 300 million people globally. Several brain regions have been linked to anxiety, but how these regions connect has been poorly understood. By exploring these connections, scientists at St. Jude Children's Research Hospital revealed that epinephrine-producing C1 neurons in mice modulate fear and anxiety. They found that while the activity of these neurons was normally temporarily elevated in times of stress, prolonged activation led to heightened anxiety that could last many days. Inhibition of C1 neurons reduced anxiety-like behaviors, suggesting these neurons may be worth exploring as therapeutic targets for anxiety disorders. The findings were published today in Neuron.

medical.title: Yeast dietary supplement may offer a safe nutritional strategy to boost cancer immunity
medical.summary: Researchers from Trinity College Dublin (TCD) and University College Dublin (UCD) have shown for the first time that a food supplement made from yeast helps the body make stronger immune cells that can fight cancer. The research teams found that adding a yeast-based supplement to the food of obese laboratory mice changes how their immune cells grow and helps make better cancer-fighting cells.

medical.title: New study highlights potential challenges for using automated AI tools in health care
medical.summary: In experiments in which physicians made decisions about treating hypothetical patients, the physicians tended to trust incorrect advice presented as being generated by artificial intelligence (AI), even after being given the opportunity to notice that patient recovery data contradicted the recommendations. Aranzazu Vinas of the University of the Basque Country, Spain, and colleagues present these findings in the open-access journal PLOS Digital Health.

medical.title: More pharmacists will be able to vaccinate children under 5. Here's what you need to know
medical.summary: More children younger than 5 should be able to be vaccinated in pharmacies from January 2027 under a range of measures designed to boost vaccine coverage announced in this year's federal budget.

medical.title: Untangling the risk factors for developing multiple chronic conditions among UK South Asian populations
medical.summary: In a study published in PLOS Medicine, Daniel Stow, Ph.D., an epidemiologist at Queen Mary University of London, UK, and collaborators discovered factors that increase the risk of developing several long-term, coexisting health conditions among South Asian populations in the United Kingdom. Since nearly 8% of the U.K.'s population has South Asian heritage, these findings lend much-needed insights for public health.

medical.title: Psilocybin therapy shows potential as a treatment for anorexia nervosa in small trial
medical.summary: A small pilot study from the Center for Psychedelic Research at Imperial College London indicates that psilocybin—the active ingredient in psychedelic "magic mushrooms"—may have potential as a treatment for anorexia nervosa when combined with therapy. Anorexia nervosa is a serious eating disorder that changes how individuals experience their bodies and typically leads to dangerously low food intake and body weight. Current treatments are limited and often ineffective, so new approaches are urgently needed.

medical.title: How random sounds played while sleeping impair memory formation
medical.summary: For several years now, sleep research has been focusing intensively on the question of whether targeted auditory stimulation during sleep can improve the consolidation of new memories. A research team in Freiburg led by the neuropsychologists Prof. Dr. Monika Schönauer and Dr. Nora Roüast has discovered that auditory stimuli during sleep can also have undesirable consequences.

medical.title: 50 million Americans live in counties without a radiation oncology clinic, study finds
medical.summary: More than 50 million Americans live in counties without a radiation oncology practice site, and millions more are at risk, according to a new study published today in the International Journal of Radiation Oncology*Biology*Physics (Red Journal), the flagship journal of the American Society for Radiation Oncology (ASTRO). The national analysis found that from 2018 to 2025, radiation oncology practice sites disproportionately disappeared from rural areas and freestanding, community-based settings, raising concerns about patients' access to cancer care in communities already facing health care and economic challenges.

medical.title: Both synthetic and organic tampons found to leach hormone-disrupting chemicals, study shows
medical.summary: A study has found some tampons leach low levels of chemicals that disrupt hormones, regardless of whether they are synthetic or organic.

science.title: Making scientific knowledge free for all
science.summary: Scientific research publishing is a particularly lucrative industry. The most recent estimates suggest that it generates around 19 billion U.S. dollars (or 16.67 billion euros) in annual turnover, with margins of around 40%. These staggering figures largely reflect the fact that the "Big Five" commercial publishers, such as Elsevier, Springer Nature, Wiley, Taylor & Francis and SAGE, are capitalizing on work that is largely funded by public money.

science.title: New physics-based machine-learning method speeds search for 2D quantum materials
science.summary: Researchers at The University of Manchester have developed a new computational approach to help identify two-dimensional materials that may host unusual quantum behavior. The work, published in Science Advances, focuses on materials with "flat bands," electronic states where electrons have very little kinetic energy. In these materials, interactions between electrons can become much more important, creating conditions linked to phenomena such as magnetism, unconventional superconductivity and topological electronic behavior.

science.title: Hummingbirds and pineapples: Why this ancient relationship hits the evolutionary sweetspot
science.summary: High above the rainforest floor, tiny ponds form in the leaves of plants perched on tree branches. Frogs breed in these ponds, alongside insects, microbes and even tiny crustaceans, creating miniature ecosystems suspended high in the canopy. These are the bromeliads—the remarkable plant family that also gave us air plants, the towering 12-meter (39-foot) "Queen of the Andes", and the pineapple on your terrible pizza.

science.title: More sustainable process for alcohol oxidation
science.summary: Researchers at Ruhr University Bochum, Germany, have developed a new method that makes the oxidation of alcohols easier to control and more sustainable. Alcohol oxidation is important both for synthetic chemistry and sustainable energy applications. Thermal processes are usually used for this purpose; in other words, the activation energy that must be overcome for the reaction is supplied in the form of heat, and oxidation takes place using oxygen under pressure.

science.title: Image: Curiosity rover sees Martian sulfur up close
science.summary: This close-up view shows fragments of sulfur crystals, the first ever seen on the Red Planet.

science.title: Aging rewires RNA production, favoring short genes over long neuronal ones
science.summary: A new Northwestern Medicine study published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences has explored the impacts of aging on essential cellular processes, findings that could shape the development of future anti-aging therapeutic strategies. Ali Shilatifard, Ph.D., the chair and Robert Francis Furchgott Professor of Biochemistry and Molecular Genetics, was the senior author of the study.

science.title: The secret life of roots: How plants fight back against salty soils
science.summary: To people, salt is a kitchen staple. But to crops, too much of it can be devastating. Across coastal regions and irrigated agricultural land, salt is accumulating in soils, making it harder for plants to absorb water and reducing harvests. Scientists estimate that around half of the world's irrigated farmland is now affected by salinity to some degree.

science.title: Quantum material opens new path for studying unusual electronic behavior
science.summary: By combining approaches from two rapidly growing fields of quantum physics, researchers at Penn State and Saint Louis University have demonstrated that a novel specialized material can naturally enable a new way to study unusual physical phenomena known as non-Hermitian dynamics.

science.title: Strengthening El Nino likely to 'rank among largest' on record
science.summary: The El Nino weather pattern picked up strength over the past month and is highly likely to "rank among the largest" ever recorded when it peaks between October and December, U.S. forecasters said Thursday.

science.title: Newly identified 'saprotropism' helps roots avoid decaying plant matter—but not animal decay
science.summary: Decaying matter shapes life in soil, but it can also create hostile zones for growing roots. Professor Jiří Friml of the Institute of Science and Technology Austria (ISTA) and international collaborators have now identified "saprotropism," a root response that guides plants away from decaying plant-derived matter—but not animal-derived decay.

science.title: Mouse found near 7,000 meters may rewrite limits of mammal survival
science.summary: A tiny mouse living nearly 7,000 meters (23,000 feet) above sea level in the Andes is helping scientists rethink the limits of life on Earth. The animal, a leaf-eared mouse, is the focus of a new international study co-authored by McMaster University researchers, revealing how mammals can survive in conditions once thought uninhabitable.

science.title: UK sets record for number of days over 34°C
science.summary: The UK on Thursday experienced its eighth day in 2026 with the mercury climbing above 34°C (94°F), the Met Office said, breaking the previous record by one day.

science.title: The hidden benefits of privacy regulation
science.summary: The digital era is characterized by near-frictionless conversion of data into dollars—absent regulatory intervention, that is. Privacy laws such as the European Union's General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) restrict corporations' use of personal information. Nonetheless, Steven Maex, assistant professor of accounting at the Costello College of Business at George Mason University and a Unanet Corporate Partner Faculty Fellow, says that "privacy regulations such as GDPR can also spur firms to make improvements that will have wider consequences, including some potential benefits."

science.title: High-throughput search tests 200 catalysts, revealing hidden routes for methane chemistry
science.summary: Catalysts are the hidden engines of modern manufacturing, directly involved in more than 80% of chemical processes. However, catalyst development is highly complex because performance is governed by the interplay of the catalyst, local operating conditions, reactant composition and product formation.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: 66 de câini, dintre care 32 erau pui, preluaţi de ASPA București de pe străzi. Cățeluș de 3 luni, salvat dintr-un canal
mix_items.summary: Echipele ASPA Bucureşti au preluat de pe domeniul pubic, de la începutul lunii iulie, 66 de câini, dintre care 32 de pui, consecinţă directă a nesterilizării şi a abandonului. Speciaţiştii avertizează că sterilizarea şi microciparea câinilor cu stăpân sunt cele mai eficiente măsuri pentru prevenirea abandonului şi reducerea numărului de animale care ajung pe domeniul public.

mix_items.title: Evaluarea Națională 2026: Tranziția către liceu, punctul critic al abandonului școlar. World Vision România propune un plan de intervenție
mix_items.summary: București, 1 iulie. Rezultatele Evaluării Naționale 2026 reconfirmă decalajele profunde dintre copiii din mediul rural și cei din mediul urban... Articolul Evaluarea Națională 2026: Tranziția către liceu, punctul critic al abandonului școlar. World Vision România propune un plan de intervenție apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

mix_items.title: Making scientific knowledge free for all
mix_items.summary: Scientific research publishing is a particularly lucrative industry. The most recent estimates suggest that it generates around 19 billion U.S. dollars (or 16.67 billion euros) in annual turnover, with margins of around 40%. These staggering figures largely reflect the fact that the "Big Five" commercial publishers, such as Elsevier, Springer Nature, Wiley, Taylor & Francis and SAGE, are capitalizing on work that is largely funded by public money.

mix_items.title: New physics-based machine-learning method speeds search for 2D quantum materials
mix_items.summary: Researchers at The University of Manchester have developed a new computational approach to help identify two-dimensional materials that may host unusual quantum behavior. The work, published in Science Advances, focuses on materials with "flat bands," electronic states where electrons have very little kinetic energy. In these materials, interactions between electrons can become much more important, creating conditions linked to phenomena such as magnetism, unconventional superconductivity and topological electronic behavior.

mix_items.title: Hummingbirds and pineapples: Why this ancient relationship hits the evolutionary sweetspot
mix_items.summary: High above the rainforest floor, tiny ponds form in the leaves of plants perched on tree branches. Frogs breed in these ponds, alongside insects, microbes and even tiny crustaceans, creating miniature ecosystems suspended high in the canopy. These are the bromeliads—the remarkable plant family that also gave us air plants, the towering 12-meter (39-foot) "Queen of the Andes", and the pineapple on your terrible pizza.

mix_items.title: A healthy diet during pregnancy associated with lower exposure to some—but not all—chemicals, study finds
mix_items.summary: Following dietary guidelines during pregnancy may reduce exposure to many—but not all—environmental chemicals from food and everyday products, according to a new study of nearly 1,500 pregnant participants. The study, "Dietary guidelines adherence and pregnancy exposure to 10 classes of priority chemicals: An observational study in the ECHO Cohort," was published in the American Journal of Clinical Nutrition.

mix_items.title: More sustainable process for alcohol oxidation
mix_items.summary: Researchers at Ruhr University Bochum, Germany, have developed a new method that makes the oxidation of alcohols easier to control and more sustainable. Alcohol oxidation is important both for synthetic chemistry and sustainable energy applications. Thermal processes are usually used for this purpose; in other words, the activation energy that must be overcome for the reaction is supplied in the form of heat, and oxidation takes place using oxygen under pressure.

mix_items.title: Hidden metastases reveal clues to colorectal cancer recurrence
mix_items.summary: Researchers identified a six-gene signature in microscopic colorectal cancer (CRC) liver metastases that may help predict recurrence after treatment. The findings suggest these tiny, often undetectable tumor deposits could serve as a tissue-based marker of residual cancer cells, recurrence risk and chemotherapy resistance.

mix_items.title: Investigating AI-based personal training
mix_items.summary: A feature News and Perspectives story on AI fitness advice has been published in the Journal of Medical Internet Research.. In "Should AI Be Your Personal Trainer?", JMIR Correspondent Anna Zucker covers the growing use of AI chatbots for personal fitness programs.

mix_items.title: Mouse study identifies C1 neurons as a driver of prolonged fear and anxiety
mix_items.summary: Anxiety disorders affect more than 300 million people globally. Several brain regions have been linked to anxiety, but how these regions connect has been poorly understood. By exploring these connections, scientists at St. Jude Children's Research Hospital revealed that epinephrine-producing C1 neurons in mice modulate fear and anxiety. They found that while the activity of these neurons was normally temporarily elevated in times of stress, prolonged activation led to heightened anxiety that could last many days. Inhibition of C1 neurons reduced anxiety-like behaviors, suggesting these neurons may be worth exploring as therapeutic targets for anxiety disorders. The findings were published today in Neuron.

mix_items.title: Image: Curiosity rover sees Martian sulfur up close
mix_items.summary: This close-up view shows fragments of sulfur crystals, the first ever seen on the Red Planet.

mix_items.title: Aging rewires RNA production, favoring short genes over long neuronal ones
mix_items.summary: A new Northwestern Medicine study published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences has explored the impacts of aging on essential cellular processes, findings that could shape the development of future anti-aging therapeutic strategies. Ali Shilatifard, Ph.D., the chair and Robert Francis Furchgott Professor of Biochemistry and Molecular Genetics, was the senior author of the study.

mix_items.title: The secret life of roots: How plants fight back against salty soils
mix_items.summary: To people, salt is a kitchen staple. But to crops, too much of it can be devastating. Across coastal regions and irrigated agricultural land, salt is accumulating in soils, making it harder for plants to absorb water and reducing harvests. Scientists estimate that around half of the world's irrigated farmland is now affected by salinity to some degree.

mix_items.title: Yeast dietary supplement may offer a safe nutritional strategy to boost cancer immunity
mix_items.summary: Researchers from Trinity College Dublin (TCD) and University College Dublin (UCD) have shown for the first time that a food supplement made from yeast helps the body make stronger immune cells that can fight cancer. The research teams found that adding a yeast-based supplement to the food of obese laboratory mice changes how their immune cells grow and helps make better cancer-fighting cells.

mix_items.title: Quantum material opens new path for studying unusual electronic behavior
mix_items.summary: By combining approaches from two rapidly growing fields of quantum physics, researchers at Penn State and Saint Louis University have demonstrated that a novel specialized material can naturally enable a new way to study unusual physical phenomena known as non-Hermitian dynamics.

mix_items.title: Strengthening El Nino likely to 'rank among largest' on record
mix_items.summary: The El Nino weather pattern picked up strength over the past month and is highly likely to "rank among the largest" ever recorded when it peaks between October and December, U.S. forecasters said Thursday.

mix_items.title: New study highlights potential challenges for using automated AI tools in health care
mix_items.summary: In experiments in which physicians made decisions about treating hypothetical patients, the physicians tended to trust incorrect advice presented as being generated by artificial intelligence (AI), even after being given the opportunity to notice that patient recovery data contradicted the recommendations. Aranzazu Vinas of the University of the Basque Country, Spain, and colleagues present these findings in the open-access journal PLOS Digital Health.

mix_items.title: More pharmacists will be able to vaccinate children under 5. Here's what you need to know
mix_items.summary: More children younger than 5 should be able to be vaccinated in pharmacies from January 2027 under a range of measures designed to boost vaccine coverage announced in this year's federal budget.

mix_items.title: Newly identified 'saprotropism' helps roots avoid decaying plant matter—but not animal decay
mix_items.summary: Decaying matter shapes life in soil, but it can also create hostile zones for growing roots. Professor Jiří Friml of the Institute of Science and Technology Austria (ISTA) and international collaborators have now identified "saprotropism," a root response that guides plants away from decaying plant-derived matter—but not animal-derived decay.

mix_items.title: Untangling the risk factors for developing multiple chronic conditions among UK South Asian populations
mix_items.summary: In a study published in PLOS Medicine, Daniel Stow, Ph.D., an epidemiologist at Queen Mary University of London, UK, and collaborators discovered factors that increase the risk of developing several long-term, coexisting health conditions among South Asian populations in the United Kingdom. Since nearly 8% of the U.K.'s population has South Asian heritage, these findings lend much-needed insights for public health.

mix_items.title: Mouse found near 7,000 meters may rewrite limits of mammal survival
mix_items.summary: A tiny mouse living nearly 7,000 meters (23,000 feet) above sea level in the Andes is helping scientists rethink the limits of life on Earth. The animal, a leaf-eared mouse, is the focus of a new international study co-authored by McMaster University researchers, revealing how mammals can survive in conditions once thought uninhabitable.

mix_items.title: UK sets record for number of days over 34°C
mix_items.summary: The UK on Thursday experienced its eighth day in 2026 with the mercury climbing above 34°C (94°F), the Met Office said, breaking the previous record by one day.

mix_items.title: Psilocybin therapy shows potential as a treatment for anorexia nervosa in small trial
mix_items.summary: A small pilot study from the Center for Psychedelic Research at Imperial College London indicates that psilocybin—the active ingredient in psychedelic "magic mushrooms"—may have potential as a treatment for anorexia nervosa when combined with therapy. Anorexia nervosa is a serious eating disorder that changes how individuals experience their bodies and typically leads to dangerously low food intake and body weight. Current treatments are limited and often ineffective, so new approaches are urgently needed.

mix_items.title: The hidden benefits of privacy regulation
mix_items.summary: The digital era is characterized by near-frictionless conversion of data into dollars—absent regulatory intervention, that is. Privacy laws such as the European Union's General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) restrict corporations' use of personal information. Nonetheless, Steven Maex, assistant professor of accounting at the Costello College of Business at George Mason University and a Unanet Corporate Partner Faculty Fellow, says that "privacy regulations such as GDPR can also spur firms to make improvements that will have wider consequences, including some potential benefits."

mix_items.title: High-throughput search tests 200 catalysts, revealing hidden routes for methane chemistry
mix_items.summary: Catalysts are the hidden engines of modern manufacturing, directly involved in more than 80% of chemical processes. However, catalyst development is highly complex because performance is governed by the interplay of the catalyst, local operating conditions, reactant composition and product formation.

mix_items.title: How random sounds played while sleeping impair memory formation
mix_items.summary: For several years now, sleep research has been focusing intensively on the question of whether targeted auditory stimulation during sleep can improve the consolidation of new memories. A research team in Freiburg led by the neuropsychologists Prof. Dr. Monika Schönauer and Dr. Nora Roüast has discovered that auditory stimuli during sleep can also have undesirable consequences.

mix_items.title: 50 million Americans live in counties without a radiation oncology clinic, study finds
mix_items.summary: More than 50 million Americans live in counties without a radiation oncology practice site, and millions more are at risk, according to a new study published today in the International Journal of Radiation Oncology*Biology*Physics (Red Journal), the flagship journal of the American Society for Radiation Oncology (ASTRO). The national analysis found that from 2018 to 2025, radiation oncology practice sites disproportionately disappeared from rural areas and freestanding, community-based settings, raising concerns about patients' access to cancer care in communities already facing health care and economic challenges.

mix_items.title: Both synthetic and organic tampons found to leach hormone-disrupting chemicals, study shows
mix_items.summary: A study has found some tampons leach low levels of chemicals that disrupt hormones, regardless of whether they are synthetic or organic.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Azi am fost foarte organizat: mi-am amanat amanarea.
